interview_name: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer

main_summary.summary: Audrey Nell Edwards Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer recount their roles as members of the St. Augustine Four, the teenage civil rights activists who in July 1963 sat in at a Woolworth's lunch counter in St. Augustine, Florida under the youth leadership of Dr. Robert B. Hayling and the NAACP Youth Council. Both speakers describe the local context, the daily picketing routine that began at St. Paul AME Church, and the July 18, 1963 sit-in at Woolworth's, McCrory's, and the service drug store with Willie Carl Singleton, Samuel White, Francis Floyd, Big Jimmy Jackson, and other youth. Arrested by Black deputy DJ Johnson and Sheriff L.O. Davis, they were sentenced under Judge Mathis after their mothers, supported by NAACP representative Mr. Shaw and lawyer Earl Johnson Sr., refused to sign a petition forbidding further demonstration. Hamilton and Ulmer served 77 days in the St. Johns County jail followed by 52 days isolation at the Florida Industrial School for Girls at Ocala; Singleton and White were sent to the Marianna reform school for boys. Released January 23, 1964, they returned to demonstrating with Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and the SCLC at the Monson Motor Lodge in June 1964, and Hamilton traveled with Ulmer to Jackie Robinson's home in Stamford, Connecticut. They reflect on later discovery of their FBI file, the Foot Soldiers Monument, and the ACCORD civil rights effort led by Gwendolyn Duncan, Barbara Vickers, and Henry and Katherine Twine.
key_themes: Dr. Robert B. Hayling's youth advisorship
Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and SCLC at Monson Motor Lodge
Jackie Robinson hosting in Stamford, Connecticut
parental refusal to sign juvenile non-demonstration pledge
erasure and recovery of St. Augustine civil rights memory
main_summary.historical_significance:
The St. Augustine Four sit-in of July 18, 1963 is one of the most consequential juvenile civil rights cases of the movement: four teenagers (Hamilton, Ulmer, Singleton, White) served lengthy sentences in Florida reform schools rather than sign a non-demonstration pledge, an act of conscience that catalyzed wider national attention to St. Augustine and helped set the stage for Dr. King's 1964 SCLC campaign there. Their testimony preserves the explicit chronology by which Dr. Hayling's local NAACP Youth Council laid the groundwork that Dr. King's SCLC then amplified, and corrects the historical record that often skips past the months between their January 1964 release and Dr. King's June 1964 arrival.

The interview also documents a deliberate civic-erasure pattern in St. Augustine: the speakers describe the city quietly demolishing civil rights landmarks (the Ponce de Leon site on US 1, the Monson Motor Lodge, attempted changes to the Slave Market), making oral testimony like this the principal surviving record. The later recognition through ACCORD's downtown plaques, the Foot Soldiers Monument, and Hamilton's Habitat for Humanity home as memorial-and-cemetery site for Singleton, Samuel White, and Ulmer's mother represents an ongoing institutional rebuilding led by Gwendolyn Duncan, Barbara Vickers, and Henry and Katherine Twine that the speakers explicitly credit.

chapters.part: Joining the St. Augustine Youth Council
chapters.title: Introductions in St. Augustine, September 2011
chapters.summary: Interviewer Joe Mosnier opens the Civil Rights History Project session at the Hilton Bayfront in St. Augustine, Florida, naming the interviewees Audrey Nell Edwards Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer along with Dr. Robert B. Hayling and the Conways. He invites the women to reflect on growing up in St. Augustine and how they became teenage activists.

chapters.part: Joining the St. Augustine Youth Council
chapters.title: Growing Up and Joining at St. Paul
chapters.summary: Hamilton remembers a St. Augustine with thriving Black businesses on Washington Street and the segregated downtown where her mother could buy school supplies at Woolworth's and McCrory's but could not drink from the whites-only fountain. Young people began gathering at St. Paul AME Church, where Mr. Clyde Jenkins, Dr. Robert B. Hayling, Mrs. Lucille Plummer, and Mrs. Fannie Fuller Wood supplied them with picket signs for daily downtown demonstrations.
related_events: NAACP Youth Council formation in St. Augustine

chapters.part: Joining the St. Augustine Youth Council
chapters.title: Deciding to Sit In, July 18, 1963
chapters.summary: On July 18, 1963 the youth decided to escalate from picketing to a sit-in, leaving their signs at St. Paul and walking downtown in pairs. Barber and NAACP member Ernest Wells gave Goldie and Big Jimmy Jackson money for the counters, and the group split among the service drug store, McCrory's, and Woolworth's, where Hamilton, Ulmer, Willie Carl Singleton, and Samuel White sat down.

chapters.part: Joining the St. Augustine Youth Council
chapters.title: JoeAnn Ulmer Joins the Youth Council
chapters.summary: Ulmer describes growing up on the west side of town "across the track," where Black children had no activities, and getting her mother's permission to stay with her sister near St. Paul AME Church so she could take part. She recalls the youth council's singing meetings with Janelle at the piano and a handful of dedicated young people who picketed every day before deciding to go in and ask for service.

chapters.part: Joining the St. Augustine Youth Council
chapters.title: Refused Service, Arrested, and Jailed
chapters.summary: When the youth asked for service they were told the counter "do not serve Niggas here" and were ordered out by Sheriff L.O. Davis and arrested by Black deputy DJ Johnson, whom Hamilton had grown up knowing. Sixteen youth were taken to the jail, the four girls and three boys separated from the rest, and they sang in their cells expecting release but were held overnight.
related_events: Arrest of the St. Augustine Four

chapters.part: Joining the St. Augustine Youth Council
chapters.title: The Petition and the Parents' Refusal
chapters.summary: In court the judge demanded that the youths' parents sign a petition pledging no further demonstration until age 21, and when twelve youth went home Hamilton, Ulmer, Singleton, and White stayed because their mothers refused. NAACP representative Mr. Shaw vowed to appeal to a higher court, and the four were sentenced to reform school after their parents held firm despite a threat to charge Dr. Hayling.

chapters.title: Dr. Hayling's Leadership and the Timeline
chapters.summary: Both speakers describe Dr. Robert B. Hayling as a youth advisor who motivated rather than restrained them and "never left our side," calling him the inspiration and father of the St. Augustine movement. Hamilton stresses the chronology, that they were released from jail on January 23, 1964, roughly three months before Dr. King's SCLC campaign arrived, work Hayling had laid the groundwork for.
related_events: Release of the St. Augustine Four

chapters.title: Faith, Song, and Reform School Suffering
chapters.summary: Hamilton and Ulmer explain that faith and uplifting songs like "I Love Everybody" let them bypass the hatred, insisting "the violence was afflicted on us but we did not afflict violence back." They recount 77 days in the county jail and 52 days in isolation at the girls' reform school scrubbing floors on their knees, and the anguish of Miss Nora, who fainted in court yet never signed the petition.

chapters.title: The Boys' Silence About Marianna
chapters.summary: Hamilton says the experience showed them they needed to do more, which is why she went back to jail months later with Dr. King. She recounts how Willie Carl Singleton and Samuel White refused ever to speak of what happened to them at the Marianna reform school, taking it to the grave, and how she later learned of the terrible things done to boys there.

chapters.title: Meeting Dr. King and Ralph Abernathy
chapters.summary: In June 1964 Dr. Hayling brought the four youth to meet Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph Abernathy in a small room, where King rose from a little red couch, hugged them, and told them they "went beyond the call of duty." Hamilton notes that their 1963 arrests came under the NAACP while their return to jail in 1964 came under King's Southern Christian Leadership Conference.

chapters.title: The Monson Motor Lodge Arrest With King
chapters.summary: At St. Mary's church Dr. King personally chose Hamilton to join his group to test the Monson Motor Lodge, alongside Reverend Shuttlesworth and a young white woman named Diane Mitchell. Owner Jimmy Brock refused to serve them, police arrested the group as a dog walked beside them, and Hamilton recalls the dog "kissing" Dr. King and King leading prayer and "I Woke Up This Morning with My Mind Stayed on Freedom" in jail before being taken to Jacksonville.

chapters.title: Jackie Robinson Hosts Them in Stamford
chapters.summary: After her release Jackie Robinson came to St. Augustine, embraced Hamilton at the Catholic church, and flew her and Ulmer first class to his home in Stamford, Connecticut. The Robinson family hosted them warmly, took them to a museum and a sandal workshop, and his daughter conducted many interviews, with photographs now held by the Jackie Robinson Foundation and young David trailing Hamilton on his horse.
related_events: Jackie Robinson's support of St. Augustine movement

chapters.title: College, Samuel White, and Jacksonville
chapters.summary: After the Civil Rights Act passed the four enrolled at Florida Memorial College, but Hamilton dropped out for lack of funds when the college relocated, while Samuel White completed about three years. Hamilton remembers White as very smart and devoted to the brotherhood; he died in Germany in 1995 and his mother had his body shipped home, and Ulmer moved to Jacksonville, where she married and stayed.

chapters.title: Kindling the Fire of Change
chapters.summary: Reflecting after a break, Ulmer frames their work as a passive, nonviolent demonstration that hurt no one and "just started the kindling of the fire," prompting Dr. Hayling to call in a larger force that finished with Dr. King and the Civil Rights Bill. She adds that the experience shaped her resolve to raise her children differently in Jacksonville and to keep speaking her mind.

chapters.title: Weighing the Cost and Lost History
chapters.summary: Looking back, Hamilton feels they accomplished little in St. Augustine, which "remains the same," and that the hardest part is how much of their history was lost even as their children gained more equal opportunity. She speaks of fighting second-class citizenship as the descendants of those who built the country, of raising her family and her son the St. Augustine High quarterback, and of the town pushing out Dr. Hayling and others who put their foot down.

chapters.title: The FBI File and a Memorial Home
chapters.summary: Hamilton reflects that her activism record limited her livelihood but was never destroyed, and that at her 2008 Habitat for Humanity house dedication historian David Nolan presented her with her FBI file, revealing they had been monitored as children aged 14, 15, and 16. She describes her home as a memorial standing beside the cemetery where Willie Carl Singleton, Samuel White, and JoeAnn's mother are buried.

chapters.title: The Foot Soldiers Monument and 2004 Recognition
chapters.summary: Asked about the Foot Soldiers Monument newly placed in the plaza, Hamilton recalls the 2004 acknowledgement of the St. Augustine Four, when their former lawyer's son Earl Johnson Jr. and Mr. Dews spoke at a celebration and the newspapers finally made their story public. She concludes that what they did was right whether or not anyone acknowledged it, because God knows their heart.

chapters.title: ACCORD, Erasure, and Somebody Will Know
chapters.summary: Hamilton credits ACCORD as the first to mark the movement downtown next to Woolworth's, praising Gwendolyn Duncan's work with former City Commissioner Henry Twine and Katherine Twine and her referral of Duncan to Dr. Hayling. She describes St. Augustine sweeping the whole movement under the rug, tearing down the Ponce de Leon site on US 1 and the Monson Motor Lodge and trying to change the Slave Market, until Barbara Vickers, Dr. Hayling, Duncan, and others resolved that somebody would know what happened there.

loc_video.caption: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Saint Augustine, Florida, 2011 September 13
